title: Group Theory for Puzzle Solving
subtitle: How mathematicians turned abstract symmetry into puzzle-cracking power

props.title: The Big Secret: Every Puzzle is a Group

children: Here's something that changed how mathematicians think about puzzles:

children.children: every puzzle with reversible moves secretly has a group structure

children: . This isn't just abstract theory — it's a practical tool that helps solve Rubik's cubes, crack combination locks, and design efficient algorithms.

children.children: But what does that actually mean? Let's build intuition from the ground up with the simplest possible examples.

A group is just a set of things (like puzzle states or moves) with a way to combine them that follows certain rules. Don't worry about the formal definition yet — we'll discover the rules by playing with actual puzzles.

props.title: Level 1: The Simplest Puzzle — A Coin

children.children: Before Rubik's cubes, let's start embarrassingly simple. A coin has two states (heads, tails) and one move (flip). That's a puzzle!

Click the coin or the Flip button. Notice how two flips bring you back to the start. This is your first taste of group structure: every element has an inverse (something that undoes it). For the flip, the inverse is... another flip!
This is the group Z₂ ("zee-two"). It's the smallest interesting group, and it appears everywhere: light switches, binary digits, even quantum computing.

props.title: Why do we call it Z₂?

children.children: The 'Z' stands for the German word 'Zahlen' (numbers). Z₂ is the integers modulo 2 — basically, arithmetic where you only care about even/odd. Adding 1+1 in Z₂ gives 0 (even + even = even... wait, 2 is even, which is 0 mod 2). Same idea as flipping twice returning to start.

props.title: Level 2: Triangle Rotation — Z₃

children.children: Now let's add a bit more complexity. Imagine a triangle with labeled corners. You can rotate it, but you can't flip it over. How many distinct positions are there?
Three rotations bring you back to the start. This is Z₃ — the cyclic group of order 3.

children: Look at the Cayley diagram on the right. It's a visual map of

children.children: all possible states

children: and how they connect. Each arrow represents applying the rotation operation. Notice it forms a cycle — that's why it's called a

children.children: cyclic

children: group!
The group elements
are:

props.title: Q: This is intriguing. Each *state* we can ...

children.children: Great observation! You've hit on something fundamental about groups. Yes, this dual nature is always the case in group theory - every element serves both as a possible state AND as an operation you can perform.

Think of it this way: when you're at state "r" (rotated 120°), you can apply the operation "r" (another 120° rotation) to get to state "r²". But "r²" is also an operation you could apply to any state. This isn't a coincidence - it's the essence of how groups work.

This is why we call them "group elements" rather than separating "states" and "operations." Each element is simultaneously both. The identity element "e" represents both "no rotation applied" and "the unchanged starting position." This elegant structure means that every action you can take is also a place you can be, and every place you can be represents an action that got you there from the starting position.
This property makes groups incredibly powerful for analyzing puzzles, because it means the set of all possible moves is the same as the set of all possible positions relative to your starting point.
Asked about "elements" — 2/7/2026, 4:58:23 PM
e

children: (identity) — do nothing, ABC stays ABC

children.children: r

children: (rotate once) — ABC → CAB

children.children: r²

children: (rotate twice) — ABC → BCA
And r³ = e (three rotations = back to start). In group language:

children.children: the order of r is 3

props.title: Level 3: Two Coins — The Klein Four Group

children.children: What happens when we combine two simple puzzles? Two coins give us something new and interesting.
This is the Klein Four Group (named after mathematician Felix Klein). It has 4 elements and a beautiful symmetric structure.

Notice something remarkable: every non-identity element is its own inverse. Flip coin 1 twice? Back to start. Flip coin 2 twice? Back to start. Flip both coins twice? Back to start. This makes the Klein Four Group very different from Z₄ (a cyclic group with 4 elements).
The Cayley diagram shows this beautifully — it's a square with diagonals, not a simple cycle. Different colored edges represent different operations.

props.title: The Group Axioms — Discovered, Not Imposed

children.children: Now that you've played with these puzzles, let's notice what they all have in common. These are the four group axioms:

steps.title: Closure
steps.description: Combining any two moves gives you another valid move. Rotate then rotate again? That's still a rotation (just more of it).

steps.title: Associativity
steps.description: (a · b) · c = a · (b · c). The order you group operations doesn't matter (but the sequence does!).

steps.title: Identity
steps.description: There's always a "do nothing" element (we call it e). It doesn't change anything when combined with other moves.

steps.title: Inverses
steps.description: Every move can be undone. For every element a, there exists a⁻¹ such that a · a⁻¹ = e.

children: These aren't arbitrary rules mathematicians made up — they're properties that

children.children: naturally emerge

children: from any system of reversible transformations. That's why groups show up everywhere!

props.title: Multiplication Tables — Your Complete Cheat Sheet

children.children: A group's multiplication table tells you everything about how its elements combine. It's like a lookup chart: find the row for your first operation, the column for your second, and the cell tells you the result.

children: Look for patterns! In Z₃, notice how each row and column contains each element exactly once. This is true for

children.children: every

children: group — it's called the Latin square property. It means you can always "solve for x" in equations like a · x = b.

props.title: Why This Matters for Puzzle Solving

children.children: So what? Why should puzzle solvers care about groups?
Group theory doesn't tell you how to solve a puzzle — it tells you what's possible.
Here's what you gain:
State counting

children: : The group order tells you how many configurations exist. A Rubik's cube has about 43 quintillion states — that's the order of its group.

children.children: Impossibility proofs

children: : Some states can't be reached from others. Group theory can prove when a puzzle is unsolvable (like a 15-puzzle with two tiles swapped).

children.children: Algorithm design

children: : Commutators and conjugates (group operations) are the building blocks of Rubik's cube algorithms.

children.children: Symmetry exploitation

children: : If a puzzle has symmetry, you can often reduce the search space dramatically.

props.title: Dihedral Groups — Rotations AND Flips

children: So far we've only seen

children.children: rotations

children: . But what about

children.children: flips

children: ? A triangle can be rotated AND flipped. The group of

children.children: all

children: symmetries (rotations + reflections) of a regular n-gon is called the

children.children: Dihedral Group D_n

When we studied Z₃, we only allowed rotations. Now we're removing that restriction — flips (reflections) are allowed too. This doubles the number of symmetries and changes the group's structure in a profound way.
D₃ — The Triangle's Full Symmetry Group
An equilateral triangle has 6 symmetries in total: 3 rotations and 3 reflections.

items.definition: Do nothing — the triangle stays exactly where it is. Rotation by 0°.
Rotate the entire triangle 120° counterclockwise. Vertices 1→2→3→1.
Flip the triangle over the axis passing through vertex 1 and the midpoint of the opposite side. Swaps vertices 2 and 3.
Flip over the axis through vertex 2. Swaps vertices 1 and 3.
Flip over the axis through vertex 3. Swaps vertices 1 and 2.

children: That's

children.children: 6 elements

children: total — matching the formula |D_n| = 2n (for a triangle, 2 × 3 = 6).

children.children: KEY INSIGHT

children: : Rotations commute with each other (r₁ ∘ r₂ = r₂ ∘ r₁). But flips DON'T commute with rotations! r₁ ∘ f₁ ≠ f₁ ∘ r₁. This makes D₃ our first
NON-ABELIAN
group — a group where the order of operations matters!

props.title: Q: Is it accurate to analogize transitive v...

children.children: Your analogy is creative but not quite accurate. Non-abelian in group theory means that the order of operations matters - if you have two group elements a and b, then a·b might not equal b·a. However, transitive verbs don't quite capture this mathematical concept.

In your example, "Tom threw the ball" versus "The ball threw Tom" aren't two different ways of combining the same elements - they're completely different statements with different subjects and objects. A better analogy for non-abelian operations would be something like putting on socks then shoes versus putting on shoes then socks. Both involve the same two actions, but the order completely changes the result.

In puzzle solving, non-abelian group operations are like move sequences on a Rubik's cube. If you do a right turn followed by an up turn (R·U), you get a different result than doing an up turn followed by a right turn (U·R). The same two moves are involved, but their order matters critically - that's what makes the cube's group structure non-abelian and what makes solving it interesting and challenging.
Asked about "NON-ABELIAN" — 2/10/2026, 10:32:08 AM

props.title: Triangle Symmetries
props.description: A triangle has 6 symmetries: 3 rotations (0°, 120°, 240°) and 3 reflections (flip over each vertex-to-midpoint axis). Crucially: doing a rotation then a flip gives a DIFFERENT result than the flip then the rotation! This is what makes D₃ non-abelian. Try composing r₁ ∘ f₁ vs f₁ ∘ r₁.

props.analogy: Abelian groups are like a polite conversation — the order doesn't matter. Non-abelian groups are like getting dressed — putting on socks then shoes is NOT the same as shoes then socks! In D₃, rotations and flips are like socks and shoes: the order you apply them changes the outcome.

props.title: Proving D₃ is Non-Abelian: r₁ ∘ f₁ ≠ f₁ ∘ r₁

children.children: Label the triangle vertices 1, 2, 3. Let's trace through two compositions:
r₁ ∘ f₁

children: (first flip over axis through vertex 1, then rotate 120°):

children.children: After f₁ (flip through vertex 1): vertex 1 stays, 2↔3 → (1, 3, 2)
After r₁ (rotate 120° CCW): each position shifts → (2, 1, 3)
f₁ ∘ r₁

children: (first rotate 120°, then flip over axis through vertex 1):

children.children: After r₁ (rotate 120° CCW): (3, 1, 2)
After f₁ (flip through vertex 1 position): vertex in position 1 stays, positions 2↔3 → (3, 2, 1)

children: Result: r₁ ∘ f₁ = (2, 1, 3) but f₁ ∘ r₁ = (3, 2, 1).

children.children: They're different!

children: Therefore D₃ is non-abelian. ∎

children.children: D₄ — The Square's Symmetry Group
A square has even more symmetries than a triangle:
4 rotations

children: : identity (0°), rotate 90°, rotate 180°, rotate 270°

children.children: 4 reflections

children: : flip over the horizontal axis, vertical axis, and two diagonal axes

children.children: Total: 8 elements

children: (= 2 × 4, following the |D_n| = 2n formula)

children.children: D₄ has even richer non-commutativity than D₃. With more elements come more ways for order to matter — more pairs of operations that give different results depending on which you do first.

props.title: Why does |D_n| = 2n?

children.children: A regular n-gon has exactly n rotational symmetries (rotate by 0°, 360°/n, 2×360°/n, ..., (n-1)×360°/n). Each of these n rotations can optionally be followed by a single fixed reflection — giving n more symmetries.

So every element of D_n is either a pure rotation or a rotation-then-flip. That's n + n = 2n elements total. In fact, you can write every element as rᵏ or rᵏf where r is the basic rotation, f is any fixed reflection, and k ranges from 0 to n−1.
This also reveals the key algebraic relation: f ∘ r = r⁻¹ ∘ f. The flip "reverses" the direction of rotation! That's the deep reason why D_n is non-abelian (for n ≥ 3).

questions.question: How many elements does D₅ (the dihedral group of the regular pentagon) have?
questions.explanation: |D_n| = 2n. For the pentagon, n = 5, so |D₅| = 2 × 5 = 10. That's 5 rotations plus 5 reflections.

questions.question: Is D₃ abelian (commutative)?
options: No — rotations and flips don't commute
It depends on which elements you pick
questions.explanation: D₃ is NOT abelian! We showed that r₁ ∘ f₁ ≠ f₁ ∘ r₁. In any dihedral group D_n with n ≥ 3, rotations and reflections fail to commute.

questions.question: What is the identity element in any dihedral group D_n?
options: There is no identity in non-abelian groups
questions.explanation: The identity element is always the 'do nothing' symmetry — a rotation by 0°. Every group (abelian or not) must have an identity element. It's one of the four group axioms!

props.title: Symmetric Groups — All Possible Rearrangements

children: We've seen rotations and reflections. Now let's meet the

children.children: ultimate shuffling group

children: — the group of

children.children: all possible rearrangements

children: of a set of objects. If dihedral groups are about the symmetries you can see in a shape, symmetric groups are about every conceivable way to scramble a collection of things.

items.definition: A rearrangement of objects. Given a set {1, 2, 3}, one permutation sends 1→2, 2→3, 3→1 (a rotation). Another sends 1→1, 2→3, 3→2 (a swap of the last two). Every permutation is a bijection — each object goes to exactly one new position, and every position gets filled.

The group of ALL permutations of n objects. Sₙ contains every possible rearrangement, making it the largest permutation group on n elements. The group operation is composition: do one rearrangement, then another.
|Sₙ| = n! (n factorial). For S₃ that's 3! = 6. For S₄ it's 4! = 24. The factorial counts every possible ordering: the first object can go to n places, the second to n−1, the third to n−2, and so on.

children.children: Think of it this way: if you have 3 books on a shelf, S₃ describes every possible way to rearrange them. With 4 books, S₄ covers all 24 arrangements. With a deck of 52 cards, S₅₂ has 52! ≈ 8 × 10⁶⁷ elements — more than the number of atoms in the observable universe!
S₃ — Rearranging Three Objects
Let's list every possible rearrangement of {1, 2, 3}. There are exactly 3! = 6 of them:
That's it — 6 elements, no more. Any rearrangement of three objects is one of these six.
S₃ = D₃! The Big Reveal

children: Look at the table above and compare it to our dihedral group D₃. The 3 rotations of the triangle (identity, rotate 120°, rotate 240°) correspond to the identity and two 3-cycles. The 3 reflections correspond to the 3 transpositions.

children.children: S₃ and D₃ are the exact same group!

props.title: Why is S₃ = D₃, and why is this case special?

children.children: A triangle has 3 vertices. Every symmetry of the triangle (rotation or reflection) shuffles those 3 vertices — so every element of D₃ is a permutation of 3 objects, meaning D₃ sits inside S₃. But D₃ has 2×3 = 6 elements and S₃ has 3! = 6 elements. Since D₃ is a subgroup of S₃ with the same number of elements, they must be the same group!

For n ≥ 4, this breaks down. D₄ has 2×4 = 8 elements, but S₄ has 4! = 24 elements. The symmetries of a square are only a fraction of all possible rearrangements of 4 objects. A square's symmetry must preserve adjacency (vertex 1 must stay next to vertex 2), but a general permutation can send any vertex anywhere.

The equation 2n = n! has only one solution for n ≥ 3: when n = 3 (since 2×3 = 6 = 3!). For n = 4, 2×4 = 8 but 4! = 24. For n = 5, 2×5 = 10 but 5! = 120. The gap grows rapidly — Dₙ becomes a tiny fraction of Sₙ. So S₃ ≅ D₃ is a beautiful coincidence that never repeats!
Cycle Notation — The Language of Permutations
Cycle notation is the compact, elegant way to write permutations. Instead of listing where every element goes, you trace the cycles — the loops that elements travel in.

props.title: Reading Cycle Notation

children: means: 1 goes to 2, 2 goes to 3, 3 goes back to 1. It's a cycle! Follow the chain: 1→2→3→(back to 1).
means: 1 and 2 swap. That's it — 3 stays put (we don't write elements that don't move).
means: swap 1↔2 AND simultaneously swap 3↔4. Two independent cycles happening at once.
or

children.children: e

children: means: nothing moves. The identity permutation.

steps.title: Step 1: Pick any element that moves
steps.description: Start with 1. Where does 1 go? Say the permutation sends 1→3.

steps.title: Step 2: Follow the chain
steps.description: Now where does 3 go? Say 3→2. And where does 2 go? Say 2→1. We're back to where we started!

steps.title: Step 3: Write the cycle
steps.description: The chain 1→3→2→1 becomes the cycle (1 3 2). Read it left to right: 1 goes to 3, 3 goes to 2, 2 goes back to 1.

steps.title: Step 4: Repeat for remaining elements
steps.description: If any elements weren't in this cycle, start a new cycle with one of them. Keep going until all moving elements are accounted for. Elements that don't move (fixed points) are left out.

children.children: Composing Permutations — Multiplication in Cycle Notation

children: To compose (multiply) two permutations, apply them one after the other and see where each element ends up.

children.children: Warning: order matters!

children: Sₙ is non-abelian for n ≥ 3.

props.title: Computing (1 2 3) ∘ (1 2) step by step

children.children: We apply (1 2) first, then (1 2 3). (Convention: rightmost acts first.)
Where does 1 go?

children: First (1 2) sends 1→2. Then (1 2 3) sends 2→3. So 1→3.

children.children: Where does 2 go?

children: First (1 2) sends 2→1. Then (1 2 3) sends 1→2. So 2→2 (fixed!).

children.children: Where does 3 go?

children: First (1 2) fixes 3→3. Then (1 2 3) sends 3→1. So 3→1.
Result: 1→3, 2→2, 3→1. That's the transposition

props.title: Now try (1 2) ∘ (1 2 3) — is it the same?

children.children: This time (1 2 3) acts first, then (1 2).
Where does 1 go?

children: First (1 2 3) sends 1→2. Then (1 2) sends 2→1. So 1→1 (fixed!).

children.children: Where does 2 go?

children: First (1 2 3) sends 2→3. Then (1 2) fixes 3→3. So 2→3.

children.children: Where does 3 go?

children: First (1 2 3) sends 3→1. Then (1 2) sends 1→2. So 3→2.
Result: 1→1, 2→3, 3→2. That's

children.children: They're different!

children: This proves S₃ is non-abelian. In fact, S₃ is the smallest non-abelian group — every group with fewer than 6 elements is abelian.

children.children: S₄ — The 15 Puzzle Connection

children: S₄ has 4! = 24 elements — every possible rearrangement of 4 objects. This is where symmetric groups start connecting to real puzzles.

children.children: The sliding 15 puzzle is secretly governed by permutation group theory!

children: Whether a scrambled configuration is solvable depends entirely on whether the permutation is even or odd.

props.analogy: Imagine you have 4 cards face-down and you want to rearrange them. Every possible shuffle — every way to pick them up and put them back — is an element of S₄. That's 24 different shuffles. Some are simple (swap two cards), some are complex (cycle all four). But here's the key: some shuffles are 'even' (can be broken into an even number of swaps) and some are 'odd'. This even/odd distinction is what makes certain sliding puzzle states impossible to reach!
props.explanation: Just like you can't un-shuffle cards with an odd number of swaps if you started with an even number (the parity is conserved), the 15 puzzle preserves parity with each tile slide. This is why Sam Loyd's famous puzzle was unsolvable.

children.children: Even and Odd Permutations

Every permutation can be written as a product of transpositions (simple swaps of two elements). The number of transpositions needed might vary, but the parity — whether that number is even or odd — is always the same for a given permutation.

items.definition: A permutation that swaps exactly two elements and leaves everything else fixed. In cycle notation: (a b). Every transposition is its own inverse — do it twice and you're back to the start.

A permutation that can be written as a product of an EVEN number of transpositions. The identity is even (zero transpositions — and zero is even!). The 3-cycle (1 2 3) is even: it equals (1 3)(1 2), a product of 2 transpositions.
A permutation that requires an ODD number of transpositions. Every single transposition like (1 2) is odd. The 4-cycle (1 2 3 4) is also odd: it equals (1 4)(1 3)(1 2), a product of 3 transpositions.
The subgroup of Sₙ consisting of all even permutations. It has exactly n!/2 elements — exactly half of Sₙ. A₃ has 3 elements: {e, (1 2 3), (1 3 2)}. A₄ has 12 elements.

props.title: Decomposing Permutations into Transpositions

children.children: 3-cycles are even:

children: (1 2 3) = (1 3)(1 2). Check: apply (1 2) first: 1→2, 2→1, 3→3. Then (1 3): 2→2, 1→3, 3→1. Net result: 1→2, 2→3, 3→1. That's (1 2 3). ✓ Two transpositions = even.

children.children: 4-cycles are odd:

children: (1 2 3 4) = (1 4)(1 3)(1 2). Three transpositions = odd.

children.children: General rule:

children: A k-cycle can be written as (k−1) transpositions. So odd-length cycles (including the identity, a 1-cycle with 0 transpositions) are even permutations, and even-length cycles are odd permutations. Yes, it's backwards — blame the math!

children.children: The 15 Puzzle Parity Theorem
Now for the payoff — the theorem that stumped a nation.
The 14-15 Puzzle: A $1,000 Unsolvable Challenge

children: In 1878, puzzle maker Sam Loyd offered $1,000 to anyone who could solve his '14-15 puzzle' — a standard 15 puzzle but with tiles 14 and 15 swapped. Thousands tried. Nobody collected. The reason? Swapping just two tiles creates an ODD permutation, and the sliding moves can only produce EVEN permutations. It's mathematically impossible — not just hard, but provably impossible!

children.children: Here's why: every legal move in the 15 puzzle (sliding a tile into the blank space) corresponds to a transposition — you're swapping the tile with the blank. A sequence of moves is a product of transpositions. Starting from the solved state, after an even number of moves you have an even permutation; after an odd number of moves, an odd permutation.

But there's a subtlety: the blank space must return to its original position for the puzzle to be 'solved.' Moving the blank around a loop and back takes an even number of moves (the blank must travel out and return). So the tile permutation must be even. Swapping only tiles 14 and 15 is a single transposition — an odd permutation — so it can never be reached from the solved state!

props.title: Parity and the Alternating Group

children.children: The solvable configurations of the 15 puzzle form exactly the alternating group A₁₅ — the group of all even permutations of 15 tiles. That's 15!/2 = 653,837,184,000 reachable states out of 15! = 1,307,674,368,000 total arrangements. Exactly half of all configurations are reachable. The other half (the odd permutations) are forever out of reach.

This is a stunning example of group theory's power: without checking every single state (over a trillion of them!), we can prove that exactly half are impossible. The proof takes a few lines of algebra, not a trillion-step computer search.

questions.question: How many elements does S₄ have?
questions.explanation: |S₄| = 4! = 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 24. The symmetric group Sₙ always has n! elements — one for each possible rearrangement of n objects.

questions.question: What is (1 2 3) ∘ (1 2) in S₃?
questions.explanation: Apply (1 2) first: 1→2, 2→1, 3→3. Then apply (1 2 3): 2→3, 1→2, 3→1. Net: 1→3, 2→2, 3→1 = (1 3). We worked this out step by step earlier!

questions.question: Is the permutation (1 2 3 4) even or odd?
options: Neither — parity only applies to transpositions
questions.explanation: A k-cycle requires (k−1) transpositions. A 4-cycle needs 3 transpositions: (1 2 3 4) = (1 4)(1 3)(1 2). Since 3 is odd, this is an odd permutation. Remember: odd-length cycles are even, even-length cycles are odd!

props.title: Subgroups and Cosets — Groups Within Groups

children: We've seen groups like D₃ and S₃ with all their elements and operations. But here's a powerful idea:

children.children: sometimes a subset of a group is itself a group

children: . These 'groups within groups' are called subgroups, and they're the key to understanding the internal structure of any group.

children.children: Think of it this way: if you have a toolkit with 20 tools, you might notice that a specific set of 5 tools is self-contained — you can do a complete category of jobs using just those 5, never needing the others. That's what a subgroup is: a self-contained subset.
What is a Subgroup?

A subgroup H of a group G is a subset that satisfies three conditions: it contains the identity element, it's closed under the group operation (combining any two elements of H gives another element of H), and every element in H has its inverse also in H. In short: a subgroup is a subset that is itself a group under the same operation.

items.definition: A subset H ⊆ G that is itself a group under the same operation as G. Must contain the identity, be closed under the operation, and contain inverses. Written H ≤ G.
A subgroup that is NOT the entire group. Written H < G. These are the interesting ones — they reveal internal structure.
The subgroup containing only the identity element: {e}. Every group has it. It's technically a subgroup, but not a very exciting one.

children.children: Finding Subgroups of D₃

Let's find ALL subgroups of our friend D₃ (the symmetries of a triangle, which is also S₃). Remember, D₃ has 6 elements: the identity e, two rotations r₁ (120°) and r₂ (240°), and three reflections s₁, s₂, s₃.

items.title: The Trivial Subgroup: {e}
items.description: Just the identity by itself. It trivially satisfies all three conditions. Order 1.

items.title: The Rotation Subgroup: {e, r₁, r₂}
items.description: The three rotations form a group! The identity is there, combining any two rotations gives a rotation (r₁ ∘ r₁ = r₂, r₁ ∘ r₂ = e, etc.), and each rotation has an inverse that's also a rotation (r₁⁻¹ = r₂). This is isomorphic to Z₃.

items.title: Three Reflection Subgroups: {e, s₁}, {e, s₂}, {e, s₃}
items.description: Each reflection paired with the identity forms a subgroup. Why? Each reflection is its own inverse (reflect twice = do nothing), so closure and inverses are automatic. Each is isomorphic to Z₂.

items.title: The Whole Group: D₃ itself
items.description: Every group is a subgroup of itself (though not a proper subgroup). Order 6.

children.children: That's it — 6 subgroups total. But why only these? Why can't we have, say, a subgroup of order 4 or 5? The answer is one of the most beautiful theorems in group theory.
Lagrange's Theorem — The Great Restrictor
Notice the orders of our subgroups: 1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 6. Every single one divides 6 (the order of D₃). Coincidence? Absolutely not.

Lagrange's Theorem: If H is a subgroup of G, then |H| divides |G|. That's it. That's the theorem. And it's one of the most powerful results in all of group theory. It instantly tells you which subgroup sizes are even POSSIBLE, without having to search for them.
For D₃ (order 6): the divisors of 6 are 1, 2, 3, and 6. So subgroups can ONLY have order 1, 2, 3, or 6. A subgroup of order 4 or 5? Impossible. Don't even bother looking.

props.title: Lagrange's Theorem in Action

children.children: For S₄ (order 24):

children: Divisors of 24 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24. So subgroups can only have these orders. You'll never find a subgroup of order 5, 7, 9, 10, 11, etc.

children.children: For Z₇ (order 7, prime!):

children: Divisors of 7 are just 1 and 7. So the ONLY subgroups are the trivial subgroup and the whole group. Groups of prime order have no proper, non-trivial subgroups at all!

children.children: Important caveat:

children: Lagrange's theorem says subgroup orders must divide |G|, but not every divisor necessarily corresponds to a subgroup. For example, A₄ (order 12) has no subgroup of order 6, even though 6 divides 12.

children.children: Cosets — Partitioning the Group

Lagrange's theorem seems almost magical — how can such a simple statement be true? The proof comes from a beautiful idea called cosets. And cosets aren't just a proof technique; they're a fundamental concept that reveals deep structure.
Given a subgroup H of G and any element g in G, the left coset gH is the set you get by multiplying g with every element of H: gH = {gh : h ∈ H}.

props.title: Cosets of the Rotation Subgroup in D₃

children.children: Let H = {e, r₁, r₂} (the rotation subgroup of D₃). Let's compute all the left cosets:
eH = {e·e, e·r₁, e·r₂} = {e, r₁, r₂}

children: — that's just H itself.

children.children: r₁H = {r₁·e, r₁·r₁, r₁·r₂} = {r₁, r₂, e}

children: — same set as eH! (Just written in different order.)

children.children: s₁H = {s₁·e, s₁·r₁, s₁·r₂} = {s₁, s₂, s₃}

children: — a completely new coset! It's all the reflections.

children.children: s₂H = {s₂·e, s₂·r₁, s₂·r₂} = {s₂, s₃, s₁}

children: — same set as s₁H.

children.children: So there are exactly TWO distinct cosets: {e, r₁, r₂} (the rotations) and {s₁, s₂, s₃} (the reflections). The cosets partition D₃ into two equal halves!

This is why Lagrange's theorem works: the cosets of H always partition G into chunks of equal size |H|. Since the chunks are all the same size and they cover all of G, the number of chunks times |H| must equal |G|. Therefore |H| divides |G|. Beautiful!
Why Cosets Matter for Puzzles
Cosets aren't just an abstract curiosity — they're a powerful tool for decomposing complex puzzles into manageable layers.

The key insight: two elements in the same coset are 'equivalent modulo the subgroup.' If your subgroup H represents rotations, then two puzzle states in the same coset differ only by a rotation. They look 'the same' if you ignore rotational differences.

props.explanation: Think of a group as a building, with each coset being a floor. Within a single floor, you can walk between any two rooms using the subgroup operations (like walking along a hallway). But to get from one floor to another, you need a fundamentally different kind of move — you need an element outside the subgroup (like taking the elevator). In D₃, the 'rotation floor' contains {e, r₁, r₂} and you move between them with rotations. The 'reflection floor' contains {s₁, s₂, s₃}. To get from any rotation to any reflection, you need to apply a reflection — the elevator between floors.

children.children: This is exactly how experts solve the Rubik's cube! They decompose the cube group into coset layers: first get the cross right (ignoring everything else), then solve the corners (modulo some subgroup), then the final permutation. Each step works within or between cosets of carefully chosen subgroups.
Normal Subgroups — When Left Equals Right

We computed LEFT cosets gH above. But you can also form RIGHT cosets Hg = {hg : h ∈ H}. In general, left and right cosets can be different! But when they're the same for every element g — that is, when gH = Hg for all g in G — we call the subgroup normal.

items.definition: A subgroup N ≤ G where gN = Ng for every g ∈ G. Written N ⊴ G. The left cosets and right cosets are identical.
When N is normal in G, the set of cosets G/N forms a group itself! The operation is (gN)(hN) = (gh)N. This only works when N is normal — it's what makes normal subgroups so special.

props.title: Normal vs. Not Normal in D₃

children.children: The rotation subgroup {e, r₁, r₂} IS normal.

children: We already found that the left cosets are {e, r₁, r₂} and {s₁, s₂, s₃}. Let's check a right coset: Hs₁ = {e·s₁, r₁·s₁, r₂·s₁} = {s₁, s₃, s₂} = {s₁, s₂, s₃}. Same set! You can verify this holds for every element: left cosets equal right cosets. ✓

children.children: The reflection subgroup {e, s₁} is NOT normal.

children: Left coset r₁{e, s₁} = {r₁, r₁s₁} = {r₁, s₃}. Right coset {e, s₁}r₁ = {r₁, s₁r₁} = {r₁, s₂}. These are different! The subgroup {e, s₁} is not normal in D₃. ✗

props.title: Why Normal Subgroups and Quotient Groups Matter

children.children: Normal subgroups let you 'collapse' a group into a simpler one. The quotient group G/N treats each coset as a single element, effectively zooming out and ignoring the internal structure of each coset.

For D₃/{e, r₁, r₂}, the quotient group has just two elements: the 'rotation coset' and the 'reflection coset.' This quotient group is isomorphic to Z₂ — and it captures the essential question: 'is this symmetry orientation-preserving or orientation-reversing?' All the detailed rotational information is collapsed away.

This is immensely powerful for puzzles. You can analyze a complex group by studying its normal subgroups and quotient groups separately. It's the group theory version of 'divide and conquer.' The Jordan-Hölder theorem says every finite group can be broken down into 'simple groups' (groups with no normal subgroups) in essentially one way — like prime factorization for groups!
The Index
The number of distinct cosets of H in G is called the index, written [G:H]. Since cosets partition G into equal-sized chunks: [G:H] = |G|/|H|.

props.title: Computing the Index

children.children: For the rotation subgroup {e, r₁, r₂} in D₃: [D₃ : {e, r₁, r₂}] = 6/3 = 2. There are exactly 2 cosets.
For a reflection subgroup {e, s₁} in D₃: [D₃ : {e, s₁}] = 6/2 = 3. There are exactly 3 cosets.
Key fact:

children: A subgroup of index 2 is ALWAYS normal! Why? There are only two cosets: H itself and 'everything else.' The left coset gH (for g ∉ H) must be 'everything else,' and so must the right coset Hg. So gH = Hg automatically. This is why the rotation subgroup of D₃ is normal — it has index 2.

props.title: Find the Subgroups!
props.description: Test your understanding: given a group, can you identify which subsets are valid subgroups? Remember the three conditions: contains the identity, closed under the operation, and contains all inverses. Use Lagrange's theorem to narrow down what orders are possible!

children.children: Challenge 1:

children: The group Z₆ = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5} under addition mod 6. Which of these subsets are subgroups? (a) {0, 3} (b) {0, 2, 4} (c) {0, 1, 2} (d) {0, 1, 3}

children.children: Answers:

children: (a) YES — {0, 3} is closed (3+3=6≡0), has identity (0), has inverses (3 is its own inverse). Order 2 divides 6. ✓ (b) YES — {0, 2, 4} is closed (2+2=4, 2+4=0, 4+4=2), has identity, has inverses (2⁻¹=4, 4⁻¹=2). Order 3 divides 6. ✓ (c) NO — 1+2=3, which isn't in {0, 1, 2}. Not closed! (d) NO — 1+1=2, which isn't in {0, 1, 3}. Not closed! Also, order 3... wait, 3 DOES divide 6. So Lagrange doesn't rule it out, but the closure check does.

children.children: Challenge 2:

children: Can Z₅ = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} (order 5) have any proper non-trivial subgroups? Think about Lagrange's theorem: 5 is prime! Its only divisors are 1 and 5. So the only subgroups are {0} and Z₅ itself. Groups of prime order are 'atoms' — they can't be broken down further.

questions.question: A group G has order 12. Which of the following CANNOT be the order of a subgroup of G?
questions.explanation: By Lagrange's theorem, the order of any subgroup must divide the order of the group. The divisors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12. Since 5 does not divide 12, no subgroup of order 5 can exist.

questions.question: In D₃, the left cosets of {e, r₁, r₂} are {e, r₁, r₂} and {s₁, s₂, s₃}. What is the index [D₃ : {e, r₁, r₂}]?
questions.explanation: The index is the number of distinct cosets. There are exactly 2 cosets: the rotations and the reflections. Equivalently, [D₃ : {e, r₁, r₂}] = |D₃|/|{e, r₁, r₂}| = 6/3 = 2.

questions.question: Which subgroup of D₃ is normal?
questions.explanation: The rotation subgroup {e, r₁, r₂} is normal because its left and right cosets are equal: it has index 2, and any subgroup of index 2 is automatically normal. The reflection subgroups {e, s₁}, {e, s₂}, {e, s₃} are NOT normal — their left and right cosets differ.

props.title: What's Next?

children.children: We've barely scratched the surface! Future sections will explore:
A mini sliding puzzle demonstrating parity arguments
Commutators — the secret weapon of speedcubers

You've now met three fundamental groups: Z₂, Z₃, and the Klein Four Group — plus the Dihedral Groups D₃ and D₄, and the Symmetric Groups S₃ and S₄! From simple coin flips to non-abelian permutations and the 15 puzzle's parity theorem, these examples contain all the core ideas you need to understand much more complex structures. The Rubik's cube group is just a bigger version of the same concepts!
